IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of incorporating sememe knowledge into distributed representations by, for the first time, jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings from HowNet, which directly targets richer semantic modeling. The attention-based variants (SAC, SAT) that select appropriate senses/sememes in context and yield interpretable embeddings, combined with reported significant gains on word similarity and analogy tasks over strong baselines, make it likely to change how researchers incorporate lexical knowledge into embeddings. The explicit limitation (treating sememes as unordered sets) tempers its scope, so it is a strong-impact contribution rather than fully transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem by, for the first time, jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet and proposes the SE-WRL framework (SSA, SAC, SAT) that yields measurable improvements on word similarity and analogy tasks. The attention-based models’ ability to select context-appropriate senses/sememes provides interpretable embeddings and better WSD, which is likely to influence work on lexical semantics and contextual representation learning. Its reliance on sememe-annotated resources (HowNet) and the simplifying assumption of unordered sememe sets limit immediate universal applicability, but the approach opens clear new research directions and is likely to be built upon in the subfield.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of incorporating sememe knowledge into distributed representations by, for the first time, jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings from HowNet, which directly targets richer semantic modeling. The attention-based variants (SAC, SAT) that select appropriate senses/sememes in context and yield interpretable embeddings, combined with reported significant gains on word similarity and analogy tasks over strong baselines, make it likely to change how researchers incorporate lexical knowledge into embeddings. The explicit limitation (treating sememes as unordered sets) tempers its scope, so it is a strong-impact contribution rather than fully transformative.

The attention-based models’ ability to select context-appropriate senses/sememes provides interpretable embeddings and better WSD, which is likely to influence work on lexical semantics and contextual representation learning. Its reliance on sememe-annotated resources (HowNet) and the simplifying assumption of unordered sememe sets limit immediate universal applicability, but the approach opens clear new research directions and is likely to be built upon in the subfield.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents solid empirical comparisons against standard baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) on word similarity (Spearman) and analogy (accuracy, mean rank) using HowNet and a Chinese analogy dataset, and reports hyperparameters. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors explicitly note a limitation (ignoring internal/hierarchical sememe relations). However, no explicit mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses is given, and code is not reported, so reproducibility and analysis of which components drive gains are only partially addressed. | B(4): The paper evaluates its models on standard intrinsic tasks (word similarity with Spearman correlation and Chinese word analogy with accuracy/mean rank), compares against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims that sememe-informed embeddings improve representations. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for the proposed SSA/SAC/SAT variants, nor broader downstream or WSD benchmark evaluations to fully validate context-sensitive claims. The authors do state a concrete limitation (ignoring sememe internal/hierarchical relations), but code availability is not mentioned which partially limits reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters.

However, no explicit mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses is given, and code is not reported, so reproducibility and analysis of which components drive gains are only partially addressed.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP venues: it addresses word representation learning, jointly models sememes/senses/words using HowNet, and extends Skip-gram with attention-based architectures (SSA/SAC/SAT). The paper uses standard datasets and evaluations (HowNet, Chinese word analogy; Spearman, accuracy) and compares to established baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe), so methods and evaluation meet venue expectations. It is well positioned for the NLP/lexical semantics community, but its dependence on a language-specific resource (HowNet) and narrowly lexical focus make it slightly less central to a general ML audience, hence not a perfect 5. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles word representation learning with sememe-enriched models (SE-WRL) and attention-based sense/sememe selection, topics of clear interest to the community. The formulation—jointly learning sememe, sense, and word embeddings using HowNet and extending Skip-gram—is appropriate, and the experiments use standard methods and evaluation practices (comparisons to Skip-gram/CBOW/GloVe/MST, Spearman for similarity, accuracy/mean rank for analogy). The paper is aimed at the right audience and is positioned correctly within NLP research on semantics and representation learning.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP venues: it addresses word representation learning, jointly models sememes/senses/words using HowNet, and extends Skip-gram with attention-based architectures (SSA/SAC/SAT). MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate embedding baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a sememe-related prior (MST), uses the same datasets and standard metrics (Spearman, accuracy, mean rank), and reports that its models significantly outperform these baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the methodological differences (three sememe-encoded strategies and attention variants) are clearly presented. The summary does not explicitly state whether the paper admits cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in acknowledging comparable prior performance. | B(4): The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and a prior sememe-related method (MST), and evaluates on the same datasets and metrics (Spearman, accuracy, mean rank) with hyperparameters reported, indicating fair experimental conditions. It also analyzes differences introduced by its three sememe-encoding strategies and attention mechanisms and reports significant improvements. The summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, and newer contextual embedding baselines are not mentioned, so comparisons are good but with minor omissions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper claims and appears to be the first to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet, which is a clear new perspective beyond standard word embedding work. It proposes three specific sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT) and integrates attention mechanisms for context-sensitive sememe/sense selection, a non-trivial extension of Skip-gram-based training. The approach and claimed empirical gains distinguish it from existing baselines (e.g., MST, Skip-gram, CBOW), so it is notably novel though it builds on established components (Skip-gram and attention). | B(4): The paper appears to introduce a new joint representation learning setup that directly learns sememe, sense and word embeddings from HowNet and proposes SE-WRL with three sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT) that integrate sememe information and attention into a Skip-gram framework. Encoding sememe-level supervision and using attention for context-sensitive sense/sememe selection is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension over standard embedding methods. However, the underlying problem (word representation learning) is well-studied, so this is a notable novel twist rather than a wholly new research problem.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper claims and appears to be the first to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet, which is a clear new perspective beyond standard word embedding work. The approach and claimed empirical gains distinguish it from existing baselines (e.g., MST, Skip-gram, CBOW), so it is notably novel though it builds on established components (Skip-gram and attention).

Encoding sememe-level supervision and using attention for context-sensitive sense/sememe selection is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension over standard embedding methods. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from introduction to methodology (SE-WRL and the three sememe-encoded models SSA, SAC, SAT) through experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (joint learning of sememe, sense and word embeddings) are stated early. Notation and methods appear consistently presented: datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameters are clearly reported. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough; no major presentation problems are indicated in the summary, so clarity is high though a few minor presentation improvements could still be possible. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from introduction to methods to experiments (describing the Skip-gram extension and three sememe-encoded strategies) and a concise conclusion. Main contributions are stated early (first-time joint learning of sememes/senses/words and the SE-WRL framework) and related work coverage is thorough; writing quality is reported as good. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and important experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are provided, so only minor presentation issues are expected.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and experimentally supported: the SE-WRL framework (SSA, SAC, SAT) using HowNet sememe annotations shows significant improvements on word similarity (Spearman) and word analogy (accuracy/mean rank) compared to standard baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST). The mathematical approach—extending Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings—is coherent and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and metrics. Minor issues that do not overturn the central claims include the absence of reported statistical significance testing and a lack of direct WSD evaluation to back the claim that attention models enable better WSD/interpretability; additionally the acknowledged simplification of treating sememes as unordered (ignoring relations/hierarchy) is a limitation. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments showing improvements on word similarity and analogy tasks against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) using Spearman and accuracy metrics, with hyperparameters reported. The proposed extension of Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe, sense, and word embeddings via three concrete strategies (SSA, SAC, SAT) is plausible and no mathematical derivation errors are indicated in the summary. However, the summary omits mention of statistical significance testing and code release, and the acknowledged omission of sememe internal relations is a methodological limitation — these are minor gaps that prevent a technically flawless rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and experimentally supported: the SE-WRL framework (SSA, SAC, SAT) using HowNet sememe annotations shows significant improvements on word similarity (Spearman) and word analogy (accuracy/mean rank) compared to standard baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST). The mathematical approach—extending Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings—is coherent and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and metrics. Minor issues that do not overturn the central claims include the absence of reported statistical significance testing and a lack of direct WSD evaluation to back the claim that attention models enable better WSD/interpretability; additionally the acknowledged simplification of treating sememes as unordered (ignoring relations/hierarchy) is a limitation.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments showing improvements on word similarity and analogy tasks against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) using Spearman and accuracy metrics, with hyperparameters reported. The proposed extension of Skip-gram to jointly learn sememe, sense, and word embeddings via three concrete strategies (SSA, SAC, SAT) is plausible and no mathematical derivation errors are indicated in the summary. However, the summary omits mention of statistical significance testing and code release, and the acknowledged omission of sememe internal relations is a methodological limitation — these are minor gaps that prevent a technically flawless rating.

main_claims: This paper, for the first time, jointly learns representations of sememes, senses and words using sememe annotations from HowNet.

It proposes the SE-WRL framework with three sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT) that incorporate sememe information and attention mechanisms for word sense disambiguation and representation learning.

Sememe information from HowNet provides semantic regularization that improves word representation learning, and the proposed models significantly outperform conventional baselines on word similarity and word analogy tasks.
The attention-based models (SAC and SAT) can select appropriate senses/sememes in context, enabling better WSD and more interpretable word and sense embeddings.
_summary.methodology: Extend the Skip-gram framework to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings by encoding HowNet sememe annotations; three strategies are proposed (Simple Sememe Aggregation, Sememe Attention over Context, Sememe Attention over Target) using attention to select senses/sememes in context.
datasets: Chinese Word Analogy dataset (Chen et al., 2015)
limitations: For simplicity the paper treats all annotated sememes of each sense as an unordered sememe set and does not consider the internal relations or hierarchical structure among sememes.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - word representation learning (word embeddings enhanced with sememes)
